summary: This source is the abstract and metadata for the 2018 Science paper introducing AlphaZero. It outlines the achievement of a single, general reinforcement learning algorithm that masters chess, shogi, and Go entirely through self-play, without relying on human domain knowledge.

principles.statement: General reinforcement learning algorithms can surpass domain-specific, handcrafted systems.
principles.rationale: By starting from random play and using self-play without human domain knowledge, a single generalized algorithm can achieve superhuman performance across multiple distinct and challenging domains.
principles.source_quote: Starting from random play and given no domain knowledge except the game rules, AlphaZero convincingly defeated a world champion program in the games of chess and shogi (Japanese chess), as well as Go.

signature_quotes.text: In this paper, we generalize this approach into a single AlphaZero algorithm that can achieve superhuman performance in many challenging games.
signature_quotes.context: Describing the leap from the Go-specific AlphaGo Zero to the generalized AlphaZero.

signature_quotes.text: Starting from random play and given no domain knowledge except the game rules, AlphaZero convincingly defeated a world champion program in the games of chess and shogi (Japanese chess), as well as Go.
signature_quotes.context: Summarizing the core achievement of tabula rasa learning in AlphaZero.

anti_patterns.description: Relying on domain-specific adaptations and handcrafted evaluation functions refined by human experts.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: While historically producing the strongest programs, these human-engineered systems are ultimately surpassed by general reinforcement learning algorithms that learn purely from self-play without human biases.
